does: This paper develops an uncertainty-based routing framework that dynamically delegates reward evaluations between a fast but potentially unreliable reward model and a slow but highly capable LLM judge. The core contribution is formulating advantage estimation in policy gradient methods as pairwise preference classification, which enables principled uncertainty quantification through prediction confidence. When the reward model expresses low confidence on a preference pair, the pair is routed to the stronger LLM judge for evaluation; confident predictions remain with the faster model. The framework is evaluated on reward model benchmarks, showing substantial improvements over random judge selection at equivalent computational cost and improvements in downstream alignment performance during RLHF training.
connects: This work addresses fundamental limitations of learned reward models in RLHF, building on prior research identifying reward hacking and distribution shift vulnerabilities in classical preference-based training. It extends recent findings that large language models themselves, when prompted for reasoning, serve as surprisingly effective preference judges without task-specific training. The paper combines these insights with uncertainty estimation techniques from Bayesian deep learning and active learning, which have long guided selective annotation and model querying. The preference classification formulation draws on existing connections between reinforcement learning and preference learning, while the routing strategy relates to mixture-of-experts and adaptive computation frameworks that conditionally allocate computation based on input difficulty.
enables: This approach opens several research directions for efficient alignment of large language models. The uncertainty quantification framework could enable more sophisticated strategies beyond binary routing, such as confidence-weighted ensemble predictions or confidence-informed prompt engineering of the LLM judge. The work points toward adaptive computation in RLHF where evaluation cost scales dynamically with input complexity rather than uniformly. Future work could explore learned routing policies that adapt based on training progress, integrate uncertainty estimates directly into policy optimization objectives, or extend the framework to other preference-elicitation schemes beyond pairwise comparisons. The ideas may also apply to other domains where expensive expert evaluation complements cheaper proxy models, including scientific discovery and complex decision-making tasks.
